steps.instruction: Confirm anterior dislocation: arm held away, 'squared' shoulder.
steps.helpDetail: Do not attempt if associated with fracture (grating sound, extreme swelling). Only for simple dislocations where evacuation is impossible.

steps.instruction: Position patient sitting up. Slowly bend elbow to 90 degrees.
steps.helpDetail: Keep the elbow tucked against the ribcage. Support the wrist. The patient must relax the shoulder muscles completely.

steps.instruction: Slowly rotate the forearm outward (externally) away from the body.
steps.helpDetail: Go extremely slow. Stop if pain increases/spasms occur. Wait for spasm to subside, then continue. Aim for 90 degrees outward rotation.

steps.instruction: If not reduced, lift arm slightly (abduction) while rotated.
steps.helpDetail: The head of the humerus often slips back into the socket with a palpable 'clunk'. Immobilize immediately in a sling.

steps.instruction: Identify the abscess: swollen, red, fluctuating bump on gum.
steps.helpDetail: Only drain if 'pointing' (visible white head) and causing severe pain/fever. Do not cut deep tissue.

steps.instruction: Sterilize a scalpel or needle tip with flame/alcohol.
steps.helpDetail: Clean the gum area with alcohol or iodine. Numb with snow/ice if available.

steps.instruction: Make a small incision at the most swollen point.
steps.helpDetail: Cut only the surface mucosa. Pus should drain immediately. Spit out pus—do not swallow.

steps.instruction: Massage gum to drain fully. Rinse with salt water.
steps.helpDetail: Keep the cut open to drain. Antibiotics are critical if available. Warm saltwater rinses every hour.

steps.instruction: Clean and dry the wound thoroughly. Stop bleeding first.
steps.helpDetail: Glue will not stick to wet/bloody skin. It seals bacteria in, so cleaning is vital. Do not use on jagged or deep wounds.

steps.instruction: Push wound edges together precisely.
steps.helpDetail: Use fingers to approximate edges. Do not let edges roll inward.

steps.instruction: Apply thin layer of glue ON TOP of the crack, not inside.
steps.helpDetail: Do not get glue inside the wound—it prevents healing. Bridge the gap on the surface.

steps.instruction: Hold shut for 60 seconds until dry.
steps.helpDetail: Add a second layer if needed. Sloughs off naturally in 5-10 days.

steps.instruction: Tie a loop of strong line/floss around the hook curve.
steps.helpDetail: Loop goes in the bend of the hook. Secure it well.

steps.instruction: Press down on the hook eye to disengage the barb.
steps.helpDetail: Push the eye down against the skin. This aligns the barb with the entry path.

steps.instruction: Yank the line sharply parallel to the skin.
steps.helpDetail: Do not pull up. Pull straight back, parallel to the skin surface. Commit to the motion.

steps.instruction: Clean wound and apply antibiotic.
steps.helpDetail: Fishhooks carry waterborne bacteria. Monitor for infection.

steps.instruction: Verify impossibility of rescue. Tourniquet base of finger.
steps.helpDetail: Only if life is at risk (fire, rising water) and digit is irretrievably pinned. Tight constriction at base.

steps.instruction: Cut skin and tendons around the bone (circumferential).
steps.helpDetail: Cut down to the bone all the way around.

steps.instruction: Locate the joint (knuckle). Bend finger to open joint gap.
steps.helpDetail: Cut through the ligaments connecting the joint. Do not try to saw through bone.

steps.instruction: Complete separation. Apply pressure dressing.
steps.helpDetail: Leave tourniquet on for max 2 hours if evacuating. Bandage stump heavily.
